heroOverview: Meningitis is inflammation of the three membranes — the meninges — that encase the brain and spinal cord, most often caused by bacterial or viral pathogens reaching the subarachnoid space through the bloodstream or from nearby structures. Bacterial meningitis strikes an estimated 1.2 million people per year globally and carries a case-fatality rate of roughly 20-30% even with modern antibiotics; survivors face permanent neurological deficits in 10-20% of cases. The classic triad of sudden severe headache, neck stiffness, and fever is present in only about half of adults, making early recognition genuinely difficult. When bacterial meningitis is suspected, empirical antibiotic therapy must begin within the hour because each hour of delay is associated with measurable worsening of outcomes. Viral meningitis, the more common form, is usually self-limiting and resolves within one to two weeks.

keyStats.prevalence: ~1.2 million bacterial meningitis cases per year globally; viral meningitis incidence estimated 5-8 per 100,000 per year in high-income countries (WHO, 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Highest incidence in children under 5 and adults over 65; adolescents and college students at elevated risk for meningococcal disease
keyStats.avgAge: All ages affected; infants under 1 year have the highest age-specific mortality
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 500,000 deaths per year from bacterial meningitis worldwide (GBD 2019)

definition: Meningitis (ICD-10: G03.9 — meningitis, unspecified) is defined as inflammation of the leptomeninges — the pia mater and arachnoid — with involvement of the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) circulating in the subarachnoid space. The code G03.9 is applied when the causative organism is not identified or documented. The condition spans a wide aetiological spectrum: bacterial pathogens (Neisseria meningitidis, Streptococcus pneumoniae, Listeria monocytogenes, group B Streptococcus in neonates) account for the most life-threatening presentations, while enteroviruses cause the majority of aseptic cases. Fungal meningitis, most commonly due to Cryptococcus neoformans, occurs predominantly in immunocompromised individuals. Tuberculous meningitis follows reactivation or primary Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection. Non-infectious causes include autoimmune disorders, drug reactions, malignancy (carcinomatous meningitis), and sarcoidosis. The neurological injury arises from bacterial toxin release, the host's inflammatory cascade, cerebral oedema, raised intracranial pressure, vasculitis of cerebral vessels, and sometimes direct cortical invasion. Management falls under neurology for diagnostic workup and ongoing care, but the acute phase is typically managed by emergency medicine and critical care. Mortality and long-term outcomes depend heavily on speed of diagnosis, the causative pathogen, patient age, and immune status.

typesClassification.description: The most dangerous form, caused most often by N. meningitidis, S. pneumoniae, or H. influenzae type b. Onset is typically rapid — hours to a day — with high fever, meningism, and, in meningococcal disease, a characteristic petechial or purpuric rash. It is fatal in roughly 20-30% of untreated cases.

The most common form overall, usually caused by enteroviruses (echovirus, coxsackievirus) and occasionally herpes simplex virus type 2. Symptoms are milder, fever is lower, and the illness usually resolves in 7-14 days without specific antiviral therapy in immunocompetent hosts.

A subacute to chronic presentation developing over days to weeks, caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Marked by basal exudate, cranial nerve palsies, hydrocephalus, and high rates of neurological sequelae; diagnosed by CSF culture, PCR, and ADA levels.

Cryptococcus neoformans is the leading cause, almost always in HIV-infected or otherwise immunosuppressed individuals. It presents subacutely with headache and confusion; diagnosed by CSF India ink preparation and cryptococcal antigen testing.
Caused by autoimmune disorders, drugs (NSAIDs, trimethoprim, intravenous immunoglobulin), malignancy, or systemic inflammatory conditions. CSF shows a white cell response but cultures are sterile.

primarySymptoms: Sudden-onset severe headache, often described as the worst headache of the person's life, reflecting raised intracranial pressure and meningeal irritation.
Neck stiffness (nuchal rigidity) — resistance and pain on forward flexion of the neck, a direct sign of meningeal inflammation.
High fever, typically above 38.5°C, often with rigors and profuse sweating.
Photophobia — painful sensitivity to light caused by inflammation of the meninges near the optic pathways.
Phonophobia — hypersensitivity to sound, accompanying the photophobia in the majority of patients.
Nausea and vomiting, often projectile in the setting of raised intracranial pressure.
Altered consciousness ranging from lethargy and confusion through to coma in severe bacterial cases.
Petechial or purpuric rash, a distinctive and alarming sign of meningococcal septicaemia that does not blanch on pressure.
Focal neurological deficits including cranial nerve palsies, limb weakness, or visual disturbance in complicated cases.
Seizures occurring in approximately 20-40% of bacterial meningitis cases, indicating cortical irritation or cerebrovascular involvement.
earlyWarningSigns: Progressively worsening headache that does not respond to standard analgesics, especially with a stiff neck beginning to develop.
Unexpected sensitivity to room lighting during what appears to be a flu-like illness.
A spotted or blotchy rash appearing anywhere on the body during a febrile illness — this requires immediate emergency evaluation.
Increasing drowsiness or confusion in someone with fever and headache.
emergencySigns: Non-blanching petechial or purpuric rash anywhere on the body — call emergency services immediately.
Rapidly declining consciousness or new seizure activity in the setting of fever and headache.
High fever with pronounced neck stiffness — this combination demands emergency assessment within minutes.
Signs of septic shock: cold extremities, mottled skin, racing pulse, and very low blood pressure.
Bulging fontanelle, high-pitched cry, and poor feeding in infants — meningitis in infants may not show classic signs.

causes.description: Gram-negative diplococcus that colonises the nasopharynx asymptomatically in 5-10% of adults and occasionally invades the bloodstream. Its lipooligosaccharide triggers a massive inflammatory cytokine storm, causing both meningitis and fulminant septicaemia with disseminated intravascular coagulation. Serogroups B, C, W, and Y dominate in high-income countries.

The leading cause of bacterial meningitis in adults. The organism spreads from the nasopharynx or from a contiguous focus such as otitis media or sinusitis, breaching the blood-brain barrier via transcytosis. Pneumococcal meningitis carries among the highest rates of neurological sequelae, including hearing loss in roughly 30% of survivors.

Coxsackievirus and echovirus account for up to 85% of all cases of aseptic meningitis. The viruses enter the bloodstream from the gastrointestinal or respiratory tract and seed the meninges. Most cases occur in late summer and autumn, are self-limited, and resolve without specific antiviral therapy.

Once the leading cause of bacterial meningitis in children, now rare in countries with routine Hib vaccination. Still important in unvaccinated populations and immunocompromised individuals. Spreads from the nasopharynx and causes meningitis primarily in those under five.

Reaches the meninges via haematogenous spread during primary infection or reactivation, seeding Rich foci at the cortical surface that rupture into the subarachnoid space. The ensuing basal granulomatous exudate damages cranial nerves, obstructs CSF flow causing hydrocephalus, and invades blood vessel walls.

An encapsulated yeast inhaled from the environment — commonly from pigeon droppings — that disseminates to the CNS in immunocompromised hosts. The polysaccharide capsule blunts the immune response, allowing slow, progressive meningeal colonisation with high CSF opening pressures causing the symptoms.

A Gram-positive rod acquired through contaminated food (deli meats, soft cheeses) that preferentially affects pregnant women, neonates, the elderly, and immunosuppressed individuals. Unique among meningitis pathogens in being intrinsically resistant to cephalosporins, requiring amoxicillin or ampicillin for treatment.

riskFactors.description: Immature immune systems in infants and age-related immune senescence in the elderly both reduce the ability to opsonise encapsulated bacteria; these age groups have the highest incidence and case-fatality rates for bacterial meningitis.

Vaccines against N. meningitidis (serogroups A, B, C, W, Y), S. pneumoniae, and H. influenzae type b dramatically reduce risk. People who have not received these vaccines — or whose immunity has waned — are at substantially higher risk.

HIV infection, haematological malignancy, solid organ or stem-cell transplantation, high-dose corticosteroid use, and complement deficiency all reduce the host's ability to clear encapsulated organisms; risk of pneumococcal and meningococcal meningitis rises 20- to 100-fold in complement deficiency.

The spleen is the primary organ for removing encapsulated bacteria from the bloodstream; individuals who have had a splenectomy or have functional asplenia (e.g. sickle cell disease) face 50- to 100-fold elevated risk of fulminant pneumococcal and meningococcal disease.

Crowded residential settings increase acquisition of N. meningitidis carriage, which can lead to invasive disease, particularly when new strains are introduced into a population of non-immune individuals.

riskFactors.factor: CSF leak or skull base fracture
riskFactors.description: A communication between the nasopharynx and the subarachnoid space gives bacteria direct anatomical access to the meninges, explaining the recurrent pneumococcal meningitis seen in patients with unrepaired CSF fistulae.

Children with cochlear implants with a positioner showed a 30-fold higher risk of pneumococcal meningitis in early data; modern implants without positioners carry substantially lower risk, but pneumococcal vaccination is strongly recommended.

Sub-Saharan Africa's meningitis belt (from Senegal to Ethiopia) experiences large epidemic waves of meningococcal disease, particularly serogroup A. Meningococcal vaccination is recommended before travel.

affectedDemographics: Infants under 12 months, who have the highest age-specific case-fatality rate
Children aged 1-4 years in under-vaccinated settings
Adolescents and young adults aged 16-25, especially in university dormitories or military barracks
Adults over 65, in whom pneumococcal meningitis predominates
Immunocompromised adults, including those with HIV, haematological cancers, or on long-term immunosuppression
Pregnant women and neonates at risk from Listeria monocytogenes
Populations in the sub-Saharan African meningitis belt
diagnosisOverview: Bacterial meningitis is a clinical-laboratory emergency. When the diagnosis is suspected on clinical grounds — typically fever, headache, and meningism — blood cultures are drawn and lumbar puncture (LP) performed without delay, unless there are signs of raised intracranial pressure, focal neurological deficits, papilloedema, or immunocompromise that mandate CT scanning first. Empirical intravenous antibiotics and dexamethasone are started at or before the time of LP, not after: guidelines are explicit that a wait for CSF results must never hold antibiotics in a patient deteriorating from possible bacterial meningitis. CSF analysis is the cornerstone of diagnosis: bacterial meningitis classically shows a polymorphonuclear pleocytosis (typically > 1,000 cells/µL), markedly raised protein (> 1.5 g/L), very low glucose (CSF:serum glucose ratio < 0.4), and organisms on Gram stain in about 60-80% of cases when pre-LP antibiotics have not been given. Viral meningitis shows a lymphocytic pleocytosis, mildly elevated protein, and normal glucose. CSF PCR panels (bacterial multiplex, enterovirus, HSV) now provide rapid pathogen identification in most centres. Meningococcal and pneumococcal antigen tests and 16S rRNA PCR increase yield when prior antibiotics have sterilised the culture. Blood culture identifies the pathogen in up to 50% of bacterial cases. Serum CRP, procalcitonin, and lactate help stratify severity. CT head is indicated before LP if there is concern about mass effect, but this should not delay antibiotics beyond one to two hours in a deteriorating patient.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive test — measures CSF cell count, protein, glucose, and culture to identify the causative pathogen and distinguish bacterial from viral or other meningitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie curled on your side or sit hunched forward. After local anaesthetic, a thin needle is passed between two lumbar vertebrae to collect several millilitres of spinal fluid. The procedure takes about 15-30 minutes and causes brief pressure or mild discomfort; a post-LP headache lasting one to two days is the most common side effect.

diagnosticTests.test: CSF Gram stain and bacterial culture
diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly visualises organisms in the CSF and grows the causative bacterium, allowing antibiotic susceptibility testing to guide therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The same CSF sample from the LP is sent to microbiology. Gram stain results are available in under an hour; culture results take 24-72 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapidly detects DNA from the most common bacterial and viral causes of meningitis — including N. meningitidis, S. pneumoniae, enteroviruses, and HSV — within 1-2 hours, especially useful when prior antibiotics have sterilised the culture.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Part of the same CSF sample is sent to a molecular laboratory; the test runs automatically and reports rapidly.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies bacteraemia, which accompanies meningitis in up to 50-70% of bacterial cases, and can yield the organism when LP is delayed or CSF cultures are negative.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw from a vein in the arm; two sets from two different sites are collected before antibiotics start.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Markedly elevated levels help differentiate bacterial from viral meningitis and assess severity; procalcitonin above 0.5 ng/mL has reasonable sensitivity for bacterial aetiology.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results return within a few hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes mass lesions, cerebral herniation risk, or hydrocephalus before LP is performed, and detects cerebral complications such as oedema or abscess.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 5-10 minute scan; usually performed without contrast for this emergency indication. A contrast-enhanced CT or MRI may follow to assess parenchymal involvement.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count and coagulation screen
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects neutrophilia (suggesting bacterial infection), thrombocytopenia, or disseminated intravascular coagulation in meningococcal septicaemia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood drawn at the same time as cultures; results within 1-2 hours.

treatmentOverview: Bacterial meningitis is a medical emergency. Empirical intravenous antibiotics — in most adults ceftriaxone 2 g every 12 hours, with amoxicillin added for Listeria coverage in those over 50, neonates, or immunocompromised patients — must begin within minutes of the clinical decision and certainly within one hour of hospital arrival. Meta-analyses confirm that each hour of antibiotic delay beyond initial assessment is independently associated with worse outcomes. Intravenous dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg every six hours for four days, given with or just before the first antibiotic dose, reduces mortality and severe hearing loss in pneumococcal meningitis in adults and H. influenzae meningitis in children. Antibiotics are narrowed to the identified organism and its sensitivities once CSF culture results are available. Viral meningitis does not respond to antibiotics; treatment is supportive — rest, adequate analgesia, anti-nausea medication, and hydration — and most patients recover fully in one to two weeks. HSV encephalitis (when encephalitis complicates herpes meningitis) is treated with intravenous aciclovir 10 mg/kg every eight hours. Tuberculous meningitis requires a four-drug regimen (rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol) for two months followed by dual therapy, typically for at least nine months total, plus adjunctive corticosteroids. Cryptococcal meningitis is treated with intravenous amphotericin B and flucytosine for induction, followed by fluconazole consolidation and maintenance. Management also includes close monitoring of intracranial pressure and neurological status in an ICU or high-dependency setting, careful fluid management, seizure prophylaxis or treatment, and chemoprophylaxis for close contacts of meningococcal cases with rifampicin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone.

medicalTreatments.name: Empirical intravenous ceftriaxone
medicalTreatments.description: Third-generation cephalosporin given 2 g IV every 12 hours in adults (100 mg/kg/day in children), covering N. meningitidis and S. pneumoniae. Combined with amoxicillin when Listeria coverage is needed. Therapy is narrowed once culture and sensitivity results are available.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mortality from pneumococcal meningitis reduced from roughly 30-40% untreated to approximately 20-25% with antibiotics and adjunctive corticosteroids; meningococcal meningitis mortality below 10% when treated promptly.

medicalTreatments.name: Adjunctive intravenous dexamethasone
medicalTreatments.description: 0.15 mg/kg IV every six hours for four days, given with or just before the first antibiotic dose. Reduces the neuroinflammatory response, cerebral oedema, and CSF outflow obstruction.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces mortality by approximately 20% relative risk reduction in adult pneumococcal meningitis (Cochrane review, 2015); significantly reduces risk of hearing loss from about 30% to about 16% in pneumococcal cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous aciclovir
medicalTreatments.description: 10 mg/kg IV every eight hours for 14-21 days when HSV encephalitis cannot be excluded or is confirmed. Aciclovir has low toxicity and high benefit-to-risk ratio, so it is started empirically whenever herpes is plausible.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces HSV encephalitis mortality from approximately 70% untreated to below 20% when started early; neurological outcomes are substantially better with prompt treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Four-drug anti-tuberculosis therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol for the first two months (intensive phase), followed by rifampicin and isoniazid for at least seven more months. Adjunctive dexamethasone reduces mortality in HIV-negative patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In-hospital mortality with combination therapy and steroids is approximately 30-40% in low-resource settings but below 15-20% in high-resource settings with intensive support; neurological sequelae occur in 30-50% of survivors.

medicalTreatments.name: Amphotericin B plus flucytosine (cryptococcal meningitis)
medicalTreatments.description: Induction with liposomal amphotericin B 3-4 mg/kg/day plus flucytosine 25 mg/kg every six hours for at least two weeks, followed by fluconazole consolidation for eight weeks and long-term maintenance in HIV-positive patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Two-week fungal clearance achieved in approximately 60-70% of patients; 10-week mortality ranges from 20-30% in HIV-positive patients on antiretroviral therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Rifampicin or ciprofloxacin post-exposure prophylaxis
medicalTreatments.description: Single-dose ciprofloxacin 500 mg or rifampicin 600 mg twice daily for two days given to household and close contacts of confirmed meningococcal meningitis to eradicate nasopharyngeal carriage and prevent secondary cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Eradicates pharyngeal carriage in approximately 97% of contacts; close-contact secondary attack rate without prophylaxis is roughly 500-1,200 times the background population risk.

surgicalOptions.name: External ventricular drain (EVD) for hydrocephalus
surgicalOptions.description: A neurosurgical drain placed through a burr hole into the cerebral ventricle to relieve acute hydrocephalus complicating meningitis, particularly tuberculous or fungal forms where CSF outflow obstruction is common.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Relieves acute obstructive hydrocephalus in most cases; long-term shunting is required in approximately 25-30% of tuberculous meningitis survivors who develop chronic hydrocephalus.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical drainage of subdural empyema
surgicalOptions.description: Craniotomy or burr-hole drainage of a subdural collection of pus, which can develop as a complication of bacterial meningitis or adjacent sinusitis or otitis. Performed when the collection is large or causing mass effect.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Mortality from subdural empyema is approximately 10-20% even with surgery; neurological outcomes are best when drainage is performed early.

alternativeTreatments.name: Supportive care and analgesia for viral meningitis
alternativeTreatments.description: Oral or intravenous paracetamol and NSAIDs, rest, adequate oral or intravenous hydration, and anti-emetics address symptoms of viral meningitis, which is self-limiting in most immunocompetent individuals.

alternativeTreatments.name: Serial therapeutic lumbar punctures for cryptococcal meningitis
alternativeTreatments.description: Removing 20-30 mL of CSF via repeated LP reduces intracranial pressure in cryptococcal meningitis, often achieving greater symptom relief than any drug alone, and is a standard adjunct to antifungal therapy per IDSA guidelines.

whySeeSpecialist: A neurologist should be involved early in any confirmed or strongly suspected meningitis case: for interpretation of complex CSF findings, management of neurological complications such as seizures, hydrocephalus, or cranial nerve palsies, and guidance on the duration and type of treatment. Infectious disease specialists add particular value in atypical presentations, treatment failure, immunocompromised patients, and suspected tuberculous or fungal meningitis. Neurosurgery is consulted for hydrocephalus requiring drainage.
doctorSelectionGuide: In the acute emergency setting, the treating team will include emergency physicians, neurologists, and often infectious disease specialists. For follow-up and rehabilitation after bacterial meningitis, seek a neurologist experienced in post-infectious neurological injury. A specialist audiology service should assess all survivors of bacterial meningitis for hearing loss within six to eight weeks of discharge. For recurrent meningitis, an immunologist should evaluate for complement deficiency or anatomical CSF leak.
preventionStrategies: Ensure vaccinations are up to date: meningococcal ACWY conjugate vaccine, meningococcal B vaccine (Bexsero or Trumenba), pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV13 in children; PCV20 or PPSV23 in adults at risk), and Hib vaccine.
For college freshmen living in dormitories, check that meningococcal vaccination (including serogroup B) is current.
Travellers to the meningitis belt or to Hajj pilgrimage receive quadrivalent meningococcal vaccine at least two weeks before departure.
Close household contacts of any confirmed meningococcal meningitis case should receive prophylactic antibiotics — ciprofloxacin or rifampicin — within 24 hours.
People with asplenia, complement deficiency, or cochlear implants should be offered pneumococcal and meningococcal vaccination and should carry emergency antibiotics.

Avoid sharing drinking vessels, cutlery, and cigarettes with individuals who have active upper respiratory illness, as meningococcal and pneumococcal transmission is facilitated by close respiratory contact.
lifestyleModifications: Complete the full prescribed course of antibiotics even if you feel better within a few days.
Arrange hearing and cognitive assessments at six to eight weeks after hospital discharge following bacterial meningitis.
Rest during the recovery phase from viral meningitis, typically one to two weeks; return to full activity when energy permits.
Inform a neurologist promptly if you develop new headaches, personality change, memory difficulties, or seizures during recovery.
Maintain vaccination records and ensure boosters are received on schedule, particularly if you have risk factors.

recommended: Adequate oral fluid intake — aim for pale urine — to prevent dehydration and support recovery
Small, frequent meals if nausea limits large food volumes during the acute phase
A well-balanced diet rich in protein for tissue repair during the recovery period
Limiting alcohol during the recovery phase from meningitis as it can worsen headache and impair neurological recovery
avoid: Alcohol and recreational drugs, which impair immune function and can worsen neurological recovery
Excessive caffeine, which may worsen headache in the subacute phase
Returning to physically or cognitively demanding activities before neurological symptoms have resolved

exerciseGuidelines: During the acute and immediate recovery phase from bacterial meningitis, bed rest is essential; physical and cognitive demands should be kept minimal. After bacterial meningitis, graduated return to activity over weeks to months is appropriate depending on neurological recovery; a rehabilitation physician or neurologist should guide this. Following viral meningitis, rest for the duration of symptoms — typically one to two weeks — and return to normal activity when persistent headache, fatigue, and photophobia have resolved. Competitive or contact sports should be avoided until cleared by a clinician.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed medications at the correct times; do not skip doses of antibiotics or antifungal agents.
Keep a symptom diary during recovery to share with your neurologist at follow-up appointments.
Monitor closely for hearing difficulties, balance problems, memory changes, or mood disturbances — common sequelae after bacterial meningitis.
Arrange post-discharge follow-up within two weeks and formal hearing assessment within six to eight weeks.
Inform close contacts promptly if you are diagnosed with bacterial meningitis so that prophylaxis can be arranged quickly.
prognosis: Outcomes from meningitis vary substantially by aetiology and speed of treatment. Viral meningitis in immunocompetent individuals has an excellent prognosis: almost all patients recover fully within one to two weeks, and mortality is below 1%. Bacterial meningitis carries a case-fatality rate of approximately 20-30% for pneumococcal and 5-15% for meningococcal disease even with modern treatment. Among survivors of bacterial meningitis, 10-20% experience permanent sequelae including sensorineural hearing loss (the most common, occurring in roughly 25-30% of pneumococcal survivors), cognitive impairment, epilepsy, focal neurological deficits, limb amputation (after meningococcal purpura fulminans), or psychosocial difficulties. Tuberculous meningitis has the worst neurological outcome profile among common forms. Cryptococcal meningitis carries a 10-week mortality of approximately 20-30% in HIV-positive individuals on antiretroviral therapy. The most important modifiable predictor of a good outcome is the interval from symptom onset to effective treatment — initiating antibiotics within four hours of presentation is associated with substantially better survival.
recoveryTimeline: Viral meningitis: most people see improvement within 48-72 hours of symptom onset and return to normal within 7-14 days, though fatigue and mild headaches can persist for a few weeks. Bacterial meningitis: fever typically settles within 2-4 days of effective antibiotics; the hospital stay is typically 10-21 days. After discharge, several months of graduated recovery should be expected, and persistent cognitive fatigue, mood changes, and hearing difficulties may take six to twelve months to improve maximally or stabilise. Formal hearing testing and neuropsychological assessment at six to eight weeks guide rehabilitation planning.
complications: Sensorineural hearing loss, the most common sequela of bacterial meningitis, affecting up to 25-30% of pneumococcal survivors due to cochlear inflammation.
Cognitive impairment and memory difficulties, which can be subtle or severe, reflecting white matter damage and cortical injury from the inflammatory process.
Epilepsy, developing in approximately 7-10% of bacterial meningitis survivors, from cortical scarring and excitatory injury.
Limb ischaemia and amputation in meningococcal purpura fulminans, where disseminated intravascular coagulation causes peripheral vascular occlusion.
Hydrocephalus from scarring of CSF drainage pathways, particularly after tuberculous or pneumococcal meningitis, sometimes requiring permanent shunting.
Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis and ischaemic stroke from meningitis-associated vasculitis of cerebral vessels.

supportResources.name: Meningitis Research Foundation
supportResources.description: UK-based charity providing patient information, support for survivors and families, and funding for meningitis research.

supportResources.name: CDC — Meningitis
supportResources.description: Overview of meningitis types, prevention, and vaccination from the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

supportResources.name: NHS — Meningitis
supportResources.description: Plain-English NHS patient guide covering symptoms, treatment, complications, and vaccination.

sources.title: van de Beek D et al. Community-acquired bacterial meningitis. Nature Reviews Disease Primers, 2016
Brouwer MC et al. Corticosteroids for acute bacterial meningitis. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2015
NICE NG240 — Bacterial meningitis and meningococcal disease: recognition, diagnosis and management (2023)
Thigpen MC et al. Bacterial Meningitis in the United States, 1998–2007. New England Journal of Medicine, 2011
Perfect JR et al. Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Management of Cryptococcal Disease: 2010 Update by the IDSA

confusedWithConditions.name: Viral meningitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Viral meningitis presents with similar symptoms but CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, and negative bacterial cultures; it is self-limiting without antibiotics.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bacterial meningitis (specified)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: When a specific pathogen is identified, a more specific code is used; G03.9 is reserved for culture-negative or pathogen-unknown presentations.

confusedWithConditions.name: Meningococcal meningitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Meningococcal meningitis is specifically caused by N. meningitidis and often accompanied by a petechial rash; when confirmed microbiologically it carries its own code.

confusedWithConditions.name: Epilepsy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Seizures from meningitis occur in an acutely ill febrile patient with meningism, whereas epilepsy presents in a non-acutely ill context without CSF inflammation.

relatedConditions.name: Bacterial meningitis (unspecified)
relatedConditions.relevance: The most serious subtype of meningitis, defined when a bacterial cause is established but no specific organism is documented.

relatedConditions.name: Viral meningitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common form of meningitis overall, caused primarily by enteroviruses; distinguished by CSF findings and its benign natural history.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculous meningitis
relatedConditions.relevance: A subacute form caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, associated with high rates of neurological complications and requiring prolonged four-drug therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Meningococcal meningitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Caused by N. meningitidis and notable for associated purpuric rash and fulminant septicaemia; largely preventable with meningococcal vaccines.

relatedConditions.name: Pneumococcal meningitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The leading cause of adult bacterial meningitis, associated with the highest rate of hearing loss and neurological sequelae among common bacterial pathogens.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of meningitis?
faqs.answer: Early signs include a sudden severe headache, sensitivity to light, neck stiffness, and a high fever. A non-blanching rash — one that stays visible when pressed with a glass — is a medical emergency. Not all three classic signs appear together, so any two of headache, stiff neck, and fever in an unwell person warrants urgent evaluation.

faqs.question: How quickly does meningitis develop?
faqs.answer: Bacterial meningitis can progress from first symptoms to life-threatening illness within 24 hours. Meningococcal disease occasionally kills within 12 hours of symptom onset. Viral meningitis typically develops more gradually over one to three days. The speed of deterioration in bacterial meningitis is why time to treatment is the most critical outcome predictor.

faqs.question: Is meningitis contagious?
faqs.answer: Bacterial meningitis caused by N. meningitidis is spread by close respiratory or oral contact — sharing drinks, kissing, or prolonged face-to-face interaction within about a metre. Casual contact does not transmit it. Viral meningitis caused by enteroviruses spreads by faecal-oral and respiratory routes. Close household contacts of confirmed bacterial meningitis receive prophylactic antibiotics.

faqs.question: How is meningitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is confirmed by lumbar puncture and CSF analysis, which shows the cell count, protein, glucose, and bacteria. Blood cultures and CSF PCR tests identify the pathogen. A CT scan is done before LP if there are signs of raised pressure. Antibiotics are started before results return when bacterial meningitis is suspected.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for bacterial meningitis?
faqs.answer: Intravenous antibiotics — usually ceftriaxone — must begin within an hour of suspicion. Dexamethasone is given alongside the first antibiotic dose to reduce brain inflammation and lower the risk of hearing loss. Patients are admitted to intensive care or a high-dependency unit for close monitoring.

faqs.question: Can meningitis cause permanent hearing loss?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sensorineural hearing loss is the most common lasting complication, affecting approximately 25-30% of pneumococcal meningitis survivors. All patients should have formal audiological assessment six to eight weeks after bacterial meningitis. Cochlear implants are an option for those with severe hearing loss.

faqs.question: What is the meningitis rash and what does it look like?
faqs.answer: The meningococcal rash starts as tiny pink or red spots or a more blotchy bruise-like pattern that does not turn white (blanch) when a glass is pressed against it. It can appear anywhere on the body, spread rapidly, and indicates bacteria in the bloodstream. This rash is a medical emergency requiring an immediate call to emergency services.

faqs.question: Is viral meningitis dangerous?
faqs.answer: Viral meningitis caused by enteroviruses is usually not dangerous in otherwise healthy people and resolves in one to two weeks with rest and analgesics. However, HSV-related viral meningitis with encephalitis needs intravenous aciclovir, and any viral meningitis in immunocompromised individuals or neonates can be severe.

faqs.question: How long does meningitis treatment take?
faqs.answer: Bacterial meningitis requires intravenous antibiotics for typically 7-14 days depending on the pathogen: meningococcal disease often needs 5-7 days, pneumococcal 10-14 days. Tuberculous meningitis needs 9-12 months of anti-TB therapy. Viral meningitis treatment is supportive and symptom-driven, typically lasting one to two weeks.

faqs.question: Can you get meningitis more than once?
faqs.answer: Recurrent meningitis is uncommon in healthy people but should prompt investigation for a CSF leak at the skull base, complement deficiency, or another immune defect. People with functional asplenia, complement deficiency, or cochlear implants face persistently elevated risk and need vaccination and antibiotic prophylaxis plans.

faqs.question: Can meningitis be prevented by vaccines?
faqs.answer: Yes. Vaccines against Haemophilus influenzae type b, S. pneumoniae (PCV13, PCV20, PPSV23), and N. meningitidis serogroups A, C, W, Y, and B are available and recommended according to national schedules. Routine childhood vaccination has reduced bacterial meningitis incidence by over 90% for covered serogroups.

faqs.question: What are the long-term effects of meningitis?
faqs.answer: After bacterial meningitis, 10-20% of survivors experience lasting effects including hearing loss, memory and concentration problems, epilepsy, coordination difficulties, limb loss (from meningococcal purpura), and mental health difficulties including anxiety and PTSD. Structured rehabilitation and long-term follow-up improve outcomes.

faqs.question: What does a lumbar puncture involve?
faqs.answer: A lumbar puncture takes 15-30 minutes. You lie on your side with knees drawn up or sit hunched forward. After cleaning and local anaesthetic, a thin needle passes between two lumbar vertebrae into the spinal fluid space. A few millilitres of CSF are collected. The main side effect is a post-procedure headache in about 10-20% of patients, treated by lying flat and adequate hydration.

faqs.question: Why are antibiotics started before lumbar puncture results?
faqs.answer: Bacterial meningitis can be fatal within hours and each hour of antibiotic delay significantly worsens outcomes. Guidelines recommend starting antibiotics at or before LP rather than waiting for culture results. Culture sensitivity is reduced by pre-LP antibiotics but PCR tests can still identify the pathogen.

faqs.question: What is the difference between meningitis and encephalitis?
faqs.answer: Meningitis is inflammation of the membranes covering the brain and spinal cord. Encephalitis is inflammation of the brain tissue itself, causing altered consciousness, behavioural change, and focal deficits more prominently. The two often overlap — meningoencephalitis — particularly with herpes simplex virus. Both require urgent assessment.

faqs.question: Who should receive prophylaxis after a meningococcal case?
faqs.answer: Household members, intimate partners, anyone who slept in the same dwelling, and healthcare workers who had unprotected exposure to oral secretions should receive prophylaxis within 24 hours — usually a single dose of oral ciprofloxacin. Public health authorities notify and coordinate this. Casual acquaintances at work or school generally do not need prophylaxis.

faqs.question: How does meningitis affect the brain?
faqs.answer: Bacteria or their toxins in the subarachnoid space trigger an intense inflammatory response that raises intracranial pressure, damages the blood-brain barrier, injures cortical neurons, inflames blood vessel walls — potentially causing stroke — and can block CSF drainage pathways, leading to hydrocephalus. This explains the range of neurological sequelae.

faqs.question: Is meningitis more dangerous in children or adults?
faqs.answer: Both extremes of age carry the highest risk. Infants under one year have the highest case-fatality rate because of immature immune systems. Adults over 65 are at elevated risk from pneumococcal disease. Adolescents face higher risk of meningococcal disease but lower overall mortality when treated promptly.

faqs.question: Should I go to hospital or call my GP first if I suspect meningitis?
faqs.answer: Call emergency services or go directly to an emergency department. Do not wait for a GP appointment. The window for effective treatment in bacterial meningitis is measured in hours, and rapid hospital assessment — including IV antibiotics and LP — cannot be provided in a primary care setting.

faqs.question: Can adults get meningococcal meningitis, or is it only in children?
faqs.answer: Adults absolutely can get meningococcal meningitis. While incidence peaks in infants and teenagers, adults over 65 also face elevated risk. Unvaccinated adults travelling to the meningitis belt, attending Hajj, or living in group quarters are at increased risk. Vaccination is recommended for all risk groups regardless of age.

metaDescription: Meningitis inflames the brain membranes causing severe headache, neck stiffness, and fever. Learn bacterial vs viral types, emergency signs, and treatment.
